g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of RISC design philosophy, a revolutionary approach to computer architecture.
g.text: Reduced Instruction Set Computer - Complete Visual Guide

g.data-narration: RISC emerged in the 1980s as a response to CISC complexity, emphasizing simple instructions that execute quickly.
g.text: RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) is a processor design philosophy that uses a
small set of simple, highly optimized instructions. Developed at IBM, Stanford, and UC Berkeley.

g.data-narration: RISC addresses CISC problems: slow simple instructions, complex decoding, and multiple clock cycles per instruction.
g.text: • Single cycle execution for most instructions

g.data-narration: RISC has five fundamental principles: single-cycle execution, load-store architecture, large register file, fixed format, and simple decoding.
In RISC, arithmetic operations occur only between registers. Memory access uses explicit load and store instructions.
All RISC instructions use fixed 32-bit format, simplifying the instruction fetch and decode logic significantly.
g.text: All instructions 32 bits → Simple decode → Fast pipelining

g.data-narration: RISC processors typically have 32 general-purpose registers, reducing memory access and improving execution speed.
Pipelining divides instruction execution into stages, overlapping processing to achieve one instruction per cycle.
g.text: Throughput: 1 instruction per cycle after pipeline fills

g.data-narration: Pipeline hazards include data hazards and control hazards. Solutions include forwarding and branch prediction.
RISC uses simple instructions pushed to software, while CISC handles complexity in hardware with microcode.
g.text: Single-cycle | 32+ registers | Compiler does hard work
Multi-cycle | Few registers | Hardware handles complexity
Modern x86 uses RISC-like cores internally, translating CISC to micro-ops

g.data-narration: ARM Cortex-M is a modern RISC microcontroller. Example shows load-store architecture with explicit LDR and ADD instructions.
RISC-V is an open-source ISA with modular extensions. RV32I base supports embedded microcontrollers through advanced vector processors.
RISC is ideal for microcontrollers due to simpler hardware, lower power, smaller chip area, and faster execution.
Key takeaways: RISC uses simple single-cycle instructions, load-store architecture, and fixed 32-bit format for efficient pipelining.
Pipeline throughput formula shows ideal speedup equals the number of pipeline stages after initial latency.
g.text: Pipeline Throughput = 1 instruction per cycle (after fill) | Speedup equals Number of Stages
